steps.title: Identify the two clauses
steps.content: Before the blank: "goats are notoriously indiscriminate" — an independent clause. After the blank: "they will devour all kinds of shrubs and weeds, leaving virtually no part of any plant unconsumed" — also an independent clause that explains what "indiscriminate" means here.

steps.title: Pick the punctuation that joins them correctly
steps.content: Two independent clauses where the second elaborates on the first call for a colon. A comma alone would create a comma splice; no punctuation would create a run-on; "and" without a comma would fuse them improperly.

steps.title: Per-choice elimination
steps.content:
• Choice A — indiscriminate and: joins two independent clauses with a coordinating conjunction but no preceding comma, producing a run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — indiscriminate,: a single comma between two independent clauses is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — indiscriminate: no punctuation between two independent clauses creates a fused (run-on) sentence. ✗
  • Choice D — indiscriminate:: the colon correctly introduces the second clause as an elaboration of the first. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
